flagSnippet: Spec is silent on a transaction carrying multiple OP_RETURN outputs
rationale: A1 itself (decode-exactly-or-no-batch) is normatively grounded, but THIS flag asks the orthogonal question of multi-anchor-per-tx / multiple OP_RETURN handling: skip-vs-reject and first-vs-all. The A-gaps section confirms no document says whether all decodable anchor outputs count, only the first, or the tx is invalid; deriving a verdict would require inventing a rule (canon Item 1 forbids). The rule text pins single-payload decode only, not output multiplicity.

attackFlagText: Spec is silent on a transaction carrying multiple OP_RETURN outputs / multiple decodable anchors: legacy extracts ALL of them and silently skips malformed outputs (root-anchor.ts:43-56). Two implementations differing on skip-vs-reject or first-vs-all fork the namespace; the hardening pass must pin 'every output independently tested, exactly the decodable ones count' or an explicit one-anchor-per-tx rule.

flagSnippet: Silent-skip means an almost-valid anchor is invisible rather than invalidating:
rationale: This flag is about near-miss / almost-valid encodings being rejected by the spec decoder — exactly what A1's normative basis pins: decode EXACTLY per the wire spec, reject truncated/trailing/wrong-magic/wrong-version. A second implementer derives 'near-miss encodings are not anchor candidates' purely from normative WIRE_FORMAT §3/§4. Unlike the multi-output flag, this needs no missing rule — it is the byte-offset/near-miss sweep the rule already proposes as tests.

docs/spec/WIRE_FORMAT.md §4 Event layouts (normative) — 'a decoder MUST reject truncated payloads (at any byte offset) and trailing bytes'; §3 Event frame (normative) — 'A decoder MUST reject any event type byte not in the registry'
docs/core/B2_VECTOR_SCHEMA.json authorityTier normative→vector-now
vectorSketch: negative — sweep of near-miss 0x0b payloads (truncated at each offset, single trailing byte, off-by-one batchSize width) mined in a confirmed tx: expected verdict reject (no batch opens, state byte-identical). Pins the spec decoder against a lenient one.
attackFlagText: Silent-skip means an almost-valid anchor is invisible rather than invalidating: an attacker can craft a payload that decodes under a buggy lenient decoder but not under the spec decoder — conformance must sweep near-miss encodings.

flagSnippet: Anchors are unsigned: a mempool observer can copy a publisher's anchor
rationale: Anchor-payload front-running is an economic/griefing analysis ('cost is the copier's tx fee, Σ-gates if gate-fee applies'), not a B2 conformance divergence for the A2 deadline-clock rule. A2's normative content (deadlines key solely off the accepted anchor's mined height) is itself testable and is covered by A2's own vectors; the copy is just another anchor evaluated by the same predicate (no identity input, A14). There is no accept/reject divergence the kernel resolves here — it is a hardening analysis duty, and the gate-fee economics are F-area, not A2.
retireReason: Not a B2 anchor-acceptance verdict divergence — it is an economic/griefing analysis of unsigned-anchor front-running; the kernel evaluates the copy by the same identity-free predicate (A14) and the cost/Σ-gate question is Gate-fee (F) area. No accept/reject fork for A2's deadline-clock rule.
sources: docs/core/B2_KERNEL_HARDENING.md A2 (lines 107-115)
docs/core/DECISIONS.md entry 47 marker-fold (ratified) — deadlines key off anchor mined height
docs/spec/ONT_ISSUANCE_FEE_MECHANICS.md §4/§8 (gate economics are F-area, front-running cost analysis)
attackFlagText: Anchors are unsigned: a mempool observer can copy a publisher's anchor payload into its own transaction and mine it earlier, starting the h+W clock before the publisher intended (grief: the batch forfeits if bytes miss h+W). The spec nowhere addresses anchor-payload front-running; hardening must analyze it (cost is the copier's tx fee, and Σ-gates if gate-fee enforcement applies to the copy).

flagSnippet: A reorg moves the anchor's mined height and therefore every deadline;
rationale: The flag explicitly states the clock is stable only under a reorg rule 'that does not exist in spec text yet.' The A-gaps section confirms reorg handling for accepted anchors (rollback/re-evaluation of anchor-derived state) has no spec text (OPEN_QUESTIONS §6 'still open: reorg handling'). da-windows S1 says h re-derives on reorg, but the kernel STATE-rollback rule itself is unwritten; deriving a verdict requires inventing the rollback semantics. Reorg re-derivation is the Z-area; the deadline-recompute-on-reorg verdict is not derivable from A2's sources today.

flagSnippet: Legacy code records batchSize for observability only and never cross-checks
rationale: A4 is verdict 'cited' on candidate-tier DA agreement §8b/§6b: the anchor commits the batch via root + batchSize and a served byte set inconsistent with the commitment (including a leaf count contradicting batchSize) does not satisfy the anchor. The batchSize-consistency reject is directly derivable from this candidate authority (candidate text is acceptable where the verdict is candidate-stays/cited). No provisional decision and no invented rule: the served-bytes WITNESS FORMAT is B3, but the kernel's fail-closed posture on a batchSize-inconsistent leaf count is derivable now — this is the 'fail-closed on absent/inconsistent evidence is vector-now even if format is B3' carve-out.

flagSnippet: Purity is only as strong as the servedEvidence definition: if the B3
rationale: D1's core rule (pure deterministic predicate, no I/O/clock/DB, identical inputs → identical verdict) is ratified and would be vector-now on its own. But this attack flag is specifically about whether two honest verifiers converge on the same 'witnessed facts' — that turns entirely on the B3 served-bytes witness being independently verifiable, a format that does not yet exist. Deriving a 'converges' verdict would require inventing the witness verifiability rule. DA agreement §8b itself places the convergence burden on the (unwritten) evidence rules.

flagSnippet: The hard case: a witness asserting an earlier first-servable height is
rationale: D14's boundary duty (the evidence layer can never override a kernel verdict; a swapped/hostile evidence impl cannot change a verdict) is ratified in SOFTWARE_CANON, and the fail-closed negative sub-cases (truncated witness, witness bound to a different anchor) are verdict-neutral and derivable. But this specific flag is the hard case: a witness asserting an earlier first-servable height is not self-verifying from chain data, so whether the kernel can validate verifiability (rather than trust the assertion) depends entirely on the B3 witness format — which does not exist. This is explicitly the design's #1 external-review ask. Deriving the verdict requires inventing the verifiability rule.

flagSnippet: Sigma g_i is only computable once the batch contents are known
rationale: The fail-closed composition requirement is derivable from the cited candidate text: F1 is a candidate-stays-grade rule whose core (fee verdict bound to F >= Sigma g_i over committed batch contents, no self-declared totals) is stated in ONT_ISSUANCE_FEE_MECHANICS §4/§8. A negative vector pinning that a fee verdict computed against a self-declared/withheld total is rejected (must compose with committed contents) is derivable now without inventing a rule. The g(name) schedule itself is B3, but the kernel's posture against self-declared totals is the F6 core, already CITED in step-3.

attackFlagText: Sigma g_i is only computable once the batch contents are known: a fee verdict issued before the DA window resolves can be gamed by withholding bytes — the fee predicate must compose with the DA verdict, never run on self-declared totals.

flagSnippet: The spec grounds the check in 'a full verifier already replays Bitcoin
rationale: The flag's actionable claim is the kernel's fail-closed posture on absent prevout-value evidence. Per the gate rules, a flag purely about fail-closed posture on absent evidence IS derivable now even though the prevout-value witness FORMAT is a B3 deliverable. F2 itself is CITED (intrinsic fee = Sigma inputs - Sigma outputs from witnessed facts, no asserted value). The proposed negative test (a prevout witness missing yields no fee fact, anchor MUST NOT count) is derivable from the system's no-oracle / fail-closed posture stated in §4/§5.

flagSnippet: Out-of-band miner payment or CPFP child fees are not part of Sigma
rationale: F2 is CITED and explicitly grounds F as the anchor transaction's INTRINSIC fee (Sigma of the anchor tx's own inputs minus its own outputs). A negative vector that a CPFP child's fee or out-of-band payment cannot count toward the anchor's gate follows directly: the computed value is over the one anchor transaction's intrinsic inputs/outputs, and §4 states the fee is intrinsic to that one transaction so it can't be reused. No invented rule needed.

vectorSketch: negative: anchor with intrinsic fee < Sigma g_i but a CPFP child / package paying the difference → rejected (only the anchor tx's own intrinsic Sigma inputs - Sigma outputs counts). Pins that package/child fees do not satisfy the gate.
attackFlagText: Out-of-band miner payment or CPFP child fees are not part of Sigma inputs − Sigma outputs of the anchor tx; the spec never states explicitly that only the anchor transaction's own intrinsic fee counts, leaving a lobbying surface ('the package paid enough').

flagSnippet: A squatter batching a million names under one tx fee is the precise
rationale: F3 is CITED. Constraint C1 (claiming k names costs ~k*g whether k solo or one batch of k; batching may amortize only blockspace, never the gate) is explicit in §2 and the acquisition state machine. A negative vector that any fee schedule giving a per-name discount as batch size grows (bulk pricing) is rejected is directly derivable; the property test (min accepted fee == N*g, linear, no volume discount) is grounded. No provisional dependency, no missing rule.

vectorSketch: negative: an anchor whose accepted fee = g + epsilon for N>1 ('one gate covers the batch') → rejected; positive: min accepted fee == N*g exactly. Pins batch-invariance (no volume discount on the gate).
attackFlagText: A squatter batching a million names under one tx fee is the precise attack this kills; any future 'bulk pricing' parameter proposal violates constraint C1 by construction and must be unrepresentable in the predicate.

flagSnippet: Miner self-issuance: a miner including its own anchor recaptures the fee
rationale: This is an explicitly accepted-and-documented residual. ONT_ISSUANCE_FEE_MECHANICS §8 states miner self-issuance is the same property Bitcoin already has, bounded by hashrate share, 'a known, bounded effect, not a break'. The flag itself says the suite should document it rather than prevent it — there is no reject verdict to pin (it is accepted behavior), so it is not a B2 conformance divergence. Matches the worklist's named example of an accepted hashrate-bounded residual.

retireReason: Accepted, documented hashrate-bounded residual (§8) — explicitly not a defendable divergence; nothing to accept/reject differently, so no conformance vector. (The F4 rule's own positive/negative tests about outputs-not-counting are separate and already covered by F4's proposed tests, not this flag.)
attackFlagText: Miner self-issuance: a miner including its own anchor recaptures the fee, issuing names at hashrate-bounded discount — spec section 8 accepts this as a known bounded residual; the test suite should document it rather than pretend to prevent it.

flagSnippet: Neither WIRE_FORMAT.md nor any kernel doc states whether one Bitcoin transaction
rationale: The flag states the predicate's verdict for the multi-anchor-per-tx case is specified nowhere. F5's own rule (a tx's fee MUST NOT be counted toward more than one anchor's gate) gives the no-double-counting half, but the flag asks whether two anchors may ride one tx at all and how fee is attributed — which the gaps list explicitly calls out as 'specified nowhere in WIRE_FORMAT.md or any kernel doc'. Deriving that verdict (legal-but-attributed vs illegal) would require inventing a rule. Spec PR needed.

attackFlagText: Neither WIRE_FORMAT.md nor any kernel doc states whether one Bitcoin transaction may carry multiple ONT events/anchors; if two anchors ride one tx, naive per-anchor fee evaluation counts the same F twice — the gate is halved. Spec must pin one-anchor-per-tx or define fee attribution.

The zero-I/O purity test (predicate signature carries no source-identity parameter) is derivable from the ratified-core T1 boundary rule (pure predicate over witnessed inputs; no adapter/source judgment) and the SOFTWARE_CANON zero-I/O lock. No provisional dependency, no missing rule — a vector asserting the predicate has no publisher-identity input channel is authorable now.

flagSnippet: 'Same bidder' is identified by bidderCommitment (sha256 of a free-text bidderId
rationale: This flag questions whether the same-bidder replacement rule carries any consensus weight at all, since bidderCommitment = sha256(free-text bidderId) is trivially evaded by minting fresh bidderIds (it constrains honest wallets, not adversaries). Whether the rule is consensus-bearing or merely a wallet convention is exactly what the adversarial pass and hardening must decide — the same unresolved question that makes the F14 rule itself non-MUST. Deriving a conformance verdict requires first deciding the rule's consensus status. Spec-blocked, same PR as the other F14 flag.

attackFlagText: 'Same bidder' is identified by bidderCommitment (sha256 of a free-text bidderId, WIRE_FORMAT §6) — a bidder trivially mints fresh bidderIds to evade the replacement rule entirely; the rule constrains honest wallets, not adversaries, and the adversarial pass should weigh whether it carries any consensus weight at all.

rationale: The ≤4 threshold being a working baseline (not frozen) is a parameterization instruction, not a verdict ambiguity. The RULE (a name at-or-below the threshold takes the mandatory bond-first path; above it the cheap-claim path) is CITED and stable across the parameter; STATUS line 53 confirms the threshold semantics. A vector parameterized on the threshold (e.g. a name at length==threshold rejects the cheap path; length==threshold+1 may finalize via cheap claim) is authorable now, with the integer flagged as launch-freeze — no provisional decision, no missing rule. rationale: The flag itself states it is Z* (reorg re-derivation) territory with 'no spec text' — it is a reorg-dangling concern, not a V-area value-record acceptance divergence. flagSnippet: Wire admits sequence 0 ('non-negative integer' per the §8 sequence-bound ruling)
rationale: Ratified Decision #17 fixes 'The first record in an ownership interval should have sequence 1 and no previous record hash,' and §8 sequence-bound (wire-normative #48) admits non-negative integers including 0. The kernel must reject a first record with sequence 0 (wire-shape-valid) explicitly. Both authorities are ratified/normative; no provisional dependency.

attackFlagText: Wire admits sequence 0 ('non-negative integer' per the §8 sequence-bound ruling) but the chain rule starts at 1 — the kernel must reject 0 explicitly rather than assume the wire layer filtered it.

flagSnippet: Gap rejection makes withheld intermediate records a liveness grief: a party hiding
rationale: The flag explicitly states the kernel verdict (reject the gapped record) is correct and that the liveness mitigation is adapter-layer (multi-resolver), 'do not fix it in the kernel.' The gap-reject verdict itself is already covered by the V7 cited rule and its gap-reject vector; the withholding-grief is a documented adapter-layer residual with no separate B2 kernel divergence.

attackFlagText: Gap rejection makes withheld intermediate records a liveness grief: a party hiding the true head blocks all successors at honest verifiers — kernel verdict is correct, mitigation is adapter-layer (multi-resolver), do not 'fix' it in the kernel.

flagSnippet: At head sequence 2^53−1 the chain freezes permanently — fail-closed but should
rationale: The verdict (no successor to a head at sequence 2^53−1 can be valid, because any +1 exceeds the §8 sequence-bound) derives from normative wire text (#48 sequence-bound, values above 2^53−1 MUST be rejected) plus V7's ratified +1 rule. The fail-closed freeze is a derivable consequence today; the flag's 'should be stated in spec text' is an editorial nicety, not a missing rule blocking the verdict.

attackFlagText: At head sequence 2^53−1 the chain freezes permanently — fail-closed but should be stated in spec text.

flagSnippet: Verifiers must recompute the head's hash from its fields, never trust a stored/declared
rationale: Ratified Decision #17 requires the successor 'point to the canonical hash of the previous destination-record statement,' and a verifier recomputing the head's hash (rather than trusting a declared value) is derivable from that plus the kernel-purity canon (inputs are record bytes, head is recomputed). The PB5 'don't trust declared hashes' lesson is legacy evidence, not the authority; the verdict stands on #17 + the §8.1 digest. No provisional dependency.

flagSnippet: Excluding the signature from the hash means the chain pins content lineage, not
rationale: V9 is candidate-stays with explicit neededSpecWork: 'DECISIONS #17 says canonical hash but no doc defines the function.' Asserting that the record hash IS the §8.1 signed digest (signature deliberately excluded) and that signature-malleability cannot fork the chain currently rests only on legacy code; deriving the verdict requires the missing spec sentence rather than reading existing authority.

Because no preserve signal is specified, fail-closed is the only derivable reading — this is the kernel's fail-closed posture on an absent/unassigned signal, derivable now even though bit-assignment is X* area.

flagSnippet: Owners can freely backdate or future-date issuedAt (the wire timestamp-form ruling
rationale: Ratified Decision #17 fixes 'Owner-issued timestamps are metadata, not the canonical ordering rule,' and the timestamp-form ruling (#48) pins issuedAt's shape, not its truth. So a record with an earlier issuedAt than its predecessor is accepted when sequence/linkage are correct, and a later issuedAt with a stale sequence is rejected. The kernel never compares issuedAt for ordering. No provisional dependency.

flagSnippet: Determinism is only relative to the servedEvidence input; DA agreement §3 admits
rationale: The flag's actual derivable predicate is the kernel's fail-closed posture on absent/unconverged evidence and its purity (no clock/network/receipt-time). Per the gate's carve-out, a flag purely about fail-closed behavior on absent evidence is vector-now even though the served-evidence WITNESS FORMAT is a B3 deliverable. SOFTWARE_CANON L2 (ratified, 'ordered, witnessed inputs in; name state out ... No DB, no network, no clock') plus DA §6c fail-closed and §6e S4 ('evidence in, verdict out; never asks did I receive the bytes') let a second implementer derive: a kernel variant that consults local receipt time (the da-convergence-sim 'naive' rule) MUST be rejected; the determinism property is accepted. The B3 part of the flag (witness-set convergence) is not the vector — the purity/fail-closed verdict is.

The kernel's anchor-time fail-closed verdict is already captured by Z1; year-ten unservability is an availability-of-history B3/launch-freeze problem with no B2 predicate the kernel evaluates differently.

Deriving a precise tie predicate would require inventing which fields participate, which steers outcomes. This is exactly the canon stop->spec-PR->code case: the equality relation must be authored before the verdict is derivable.

flagSnippet: The spec defines eligibility for the confirmed root but never defines the tip-side
rationale: DA §6a/§6e define the CONFIRMED-root eligibility (K-deep) and the kernel can derive that a sub-K anchor contributes nothing to the confirmed root (that is the rule Z4 itself, provisional on da-windows). But this flag is specifically about the PROVISIONAL (sub-K, tip-side) state view — what a verifier may assert before K-depth and how it behaves under reorg. The Gaps section is explicit: 'No spec text defines the provisional (sub-K, tip-side) state view at all ... is entirely unwritten (only product-tier state labels in AUCTION.md's Provisional Utility table).' Deriving a 'presents provisional as final' verdict requires inventing the tip-side rule.

attackFlagText: The spec defines eligibility for the confirmed root but never defines the tip-side provisional view's reorg behavior or forbids acting on sub-K state — a surface that presents provisional state as final games the boundary without violating the letter.

flagSnippet: K is unpinned (da-windows); a small K re-exposes the §3 boundary divergence
rationale: Z4's core verdict — an anchor at depth K-1 contributes nothing to the confirmed root; at depth K it does; confirmed-root sequence invariant under sub-K reorgs — is derivable from DA §6a + §6e (S2 eligibleAt H >= h+K), but that algebra is provisional on da-windows (#49, PROVISIONAL pending DK). K being a per-network parameter passed as input (S5), not a baked constant, is itself the #49 ruling. So the verdict is derivable but rides on the provisional decision; the consensus-criticality of K-choice is exactly the #49 parameters-as-inputs point.

attackFlagText: K is unpinned (da-windows); a small K re-exposes the §3 boundary divergence the lag exists to absorb — parameter choice is consensus-critical, not tuning.

flagSnippet: The invariance claim is scoped to disjoint insertions only; quoting it unscoped
rationale: Z6 (permutation-invariance of the root over DISJOINT-leaf batch insertions) is grounded in DA §5 ('Disjoint insertions commute ... the root is a function of the set of leaves, not the order') and SOFTWARE_CANON Item 5 B2 gate (ratified, 'property tests over event orderings (reorg/permutation invariance where the spec claims it)'). The flag is a SCOPE-pinning note, not a missing rule: the vector must test permutation-invariance ONLY for disjoint insertions and must NOT extend it to same-name conflicts (which Z7 resolves by commit priority). The scope boundary is fully derivable from the candidate text + ratified gate, with no dependence on #49/#50.

flagSnippet: Same-block tie gaming by fee/miner ordering is real but valueless for acquisition
rationale: RISKS.md (tie-break gaming row) documents this as 'Low value ... an auction is opened by a bond, never by a claim alone ... The tie-break is only a delta-determinism floor for merge ordering' — an accepted, documented residual that rests on the RATIFIED bond-opens rule (#37). The flag's own conditional ('IF bond-opens ever weakens, this silently becomes an award rule') is a hypothetical contingent on un-ratifying a ratified decision; bond-opens (#37) is ratified, not provisional, so there is no live B2 divergence. The Z7 award-vs-order distinction itself is covered by the Z7 collision-nullify negative test.

retireReason: Accepted+documented residual (RISKS.md tie-break gaming = delta-determinism floor only) resting on ratified bond-opens (#37); the flag is a hypothetical contingent on un-ratifying a ratified decision, no live B2 divergence — and the award-vs-order scope is already guarded by the Z7 collision-nullify negative.
attackFlagText: Same-block tie gaming by fee/miner ordering is real but valueless for acquisition per RISKS ('only a delta-determinism floor') — yet that bound itself rests on the bond-opens rule; if bond-opens ever weakens, this tie-break silently becomes an award rule.

rationale: Whether, at the exact boundary where two claims' windows do not overlap by one block, first-anchor-wins applies or the collision/nullify path engages is UNSTATED. GLOSSARY first-anchor-wins covers 'outside a live window' and 'Ordering inside a window never awards a contested name,' but the precise window-overlap boundary predicate is undefined — and the windows themselves are da-windows-parameterized AND the boundary semantics are unwritten. Deriving the boundary verdict requires inventing the overlap predicate; this is also the Z7 'Needed spec work' (tuple + merge section) plus the window-boundary hole.

flagSnippet: Window-expiry boundary vs reorg: a bond in the window's last block can reorg out
rationale: Z9's core (notice-window outcome derived from current-chain claims/bonds, never latched, reorg changes outcome identically) is provisional on da-windows. But this flag exposes a hole the algebra does NOT close: nothing ties W_notice expiry to the K-depth stability boundary, so 'final' has no stated STABILIZATION DEPTH. The Gaps section confirms: 'No spec text ties the non-DA clocks — notice window expiry ... — to the K-depth reorg-stability boundary: at what depth a final/nullified verdict stops being reorg-mutable is nowhere stated.' DECISIONS Open Q6 lists reorg handling as still open. Deriving the stabilization depth requires inventing it.

The bond's qualification reads re-derived current-chain mined height, not first-seen. This is derivable but the algebra is provisional on da-windows (#49). The 'same reorg-to-extend family as Z5' note is the spec-blocked re-mine-priority piece (separate flag), but the basic current-chain-vs-first-seen choice flows from #49 S1.

flagSnippet: adaptive_extension(...) has no defined input set: if it consumes anything other than canonical-chain facts
rationale: adaptive_extension(...) has NO defined input set. The Z11 'Needed spec work' is explicit: 'adaptive_extension's permitted inputs must be specified as deterministic chain facts before the kernel can host it at all.' Without that, you cannot derive whether a given extension is valid (purity) — deriving it requires inventing the permitted-input set. Note: the EXTEND-ONLY / no-shrink half of Z11 is separately derivable (the shrink-reject test, see other flag), but THIS flag is the undefined-input hole, which is spec-blocked.

specPr: AUCTION.md Decay Rule spec PR: specify adaptive_extension's permitted input set as deterministic canonical-chain facts only (and exclude manipulable chain-visible signals), so a given extension's validity / kernel-purity verdict is derivable.
attackFlagText: adaptive_extension(...) has no defined input set: if it consumes anything other than canonical-chain facts it violates kernel purity (canon: no clock, no network) and breaks Z1; if it consumes manipulable chain-visible signals, an adversary can extend windows to lock competitors' capital — extend-only bounds the damage direction but not the grief.

flagSnippet: issuedAt is normative wire content but semantically untethered: any future rule that gives it meaning
rationale: Z12 (no kernel predicate consumes wall-clock; off-chain timestamps like issuedAt are opaque committed bytes, never compared to 'now') is derivable from SOFTWARE_CANON L2 (ratified, 'No DB, no network, no clock') + WIRE_FORMAT §8 (normative, wire-normative #48: 'Every issuedAt is the literal form'). The flag is a forward-guard note (a future rule giving issuedAt freshness/expiry semantics would re-import wall-clock); the testable verdict today is: a value-record or recovery rule that accepts/rejects by comparing issuedAt to current time MUST fail; issuedAt influences only digest bytes. Fully grounded in ratified + normative text, no #49/#50 dependency.

flagSnippet: If only W ≤ K is ratified (without K ≥ W + C), the challenge window C can extend past K-depth
rationale: Z13 was RESTATED in step-3 per da-windows (#49): DA §6e S6 pins the strong K >= W + C invariant (the weak W <= K is 'implied by, and superseded by, the strong form'), and S5 makes (K,W,C) inputs (no baked constant). The flag's hazard (weak-form-only lets C extend past K-depth so a batch becomes confirmed-root-eligible while its fail-closed challenge is still open) is exactly the ordering bug the strong invariant forecloses — derivable from §6e, but §6e is da-windows (#49) which is PROVISIONAL pending DK. So the S6-reject vectors (K = W+C-1 rejected, etc.) and dual-parameterization vectors are derivable but ride on #49. This is NOT retired-by-restatement: the restatement made it derivable but provisional, and the S6-reject + dual-param vectors land in step 4 per #49 S7.

rationale: S6 is cited. The funding-key 'compromise' is an accepted property of bond continuity, not a missing rule.

Not derivable now.

flagSnippet: BIP322 verification embeds Bitcoin script semantics — the B2 purity gate
rationale: R9 is restated per recovery-auth (#50) and 'Blocked on decision: recovery-auth'. The flag is the purity requirement: any BIP322 verification must be a deterministic pure function over (address, message, signature), no network/UTXO lookups, or recovery verdicts stop being replayable. The purity verdict itself derives from ratified SOFTWARE_CANON; BUT under #50 (b1) the BIP322 wallet proof is non-authorizing corroboration (its absence/failure must not block, presence must not substitute), whereas under the standing counter-design b2h the BIP322 verifier IS inside the audited kernel as an authorizing input. Whether BIP322 verification is even an acceptance-path predicate hinges on #50, so the vector's expected shape is provisional.
vectorSketch: negative: a VALID wallet proof + invalid event BIP340 signature => still reject (corroboration cannot substitute for the on-chain signature); positive (purity): identical (address,message,signature) yields byte-identical BIP322 verdict, no I/O. Flip (b2h): the BIP322 verifier becomes an authorizing kernel predicate (descriptor + wallet-proof evidence + BIP322/script/text verification inside the audited kernel), so absence/failure of the proof DOES block acceptance.

rationale: Second R10 flag: divergence between normative §4.2 naming (signature, 64, Schnorr) and the only deployed behavior (legacy filled the slot with proofHash || 32 reserved zero bytes — a commitment, not a signature). rationale: This flag is the determinism requirement: completion must be a deterministic function of chain height with no dependence on when the derived-state refresh is invoked (legacy keys it on a caller-supplied currentHeight). That purity/determinism verdict derives from ratified SOFTWARE_CANON (kernel = pure deterministic predicate over witnessed inputs incl. chain facts; no clock; identical inputs => identical verdict). It does not depend on the precise completion-transition spec gap (new interval ref etc., which is R18's separate Needed spec work) — the determinism property is testable now (replay same blocks in one pass vs incrementally => identical post-completion state). No #49/#50 dependency. Per the gate, determinism/purity posture is derivable now even where adjacent format work is deferred.

rationale: R19's core purity/fail-closed rule is cited (SOFTWARE_CANON) and the empty-evidence-rejected property is derivable. But THIS flag is specifically the evidence-timing question — 'available as of WHEN?' — requiring 'the evidence set pinned to a defined observation rule, or proof-withholding/late-publication games decide outcomes.' The Gaps section lists 'Evidence-timing rule for recovery evidence' (by when must the descriptor and wallet proof be served/witnessed relative to the invoke's mined height) as having no spec text, with late-proof replay explicitly unimplemented. (The bare fail-closed-on-absent-evidence posture is already captured by R1/R19 cited core; this timing flag is the unresolved residue.)

rationale: This flag is the benign mature-path posture: under X8 the kernel MUST NOT impose any bond conjunct on a mature transfer, so the signed successorBondVout byte carries no semantic effect and any value (including out-of-range) is ignored. The 'ignored' verdict is directly derivable from DECISIONS #4/#27 and the candidate state-machine ('mature transfers do not require successor bond continuity') plus LAUNCH ('no successor bond is required') — the flag's note that the spec 'should say ignored' is a wording recommendation, not a verdict gap.

flagSnippet: Free-floating authorization extraction: the §5 digest excludes the carrying txid,
rationale: The flag describes a deliberately accepted, documented residual: the §5 digest excludes the carrying txid by design, the kernel cannot detect underpayment, and DECISIONS #30 explicitly assigns the binding defense to the wallet/B5 transfer flow (cooperative PSBT + seller-controlled input). This is not a B2 conformance divergence — it is the kernel's intended payment-blindness with the mitigation pushed to B5. The B2 predicate (X10 positive: a mature transfer paying the seller nothing still applies) is already covered by X10's own positive tests; the underpayment binding itself is a B5 deliverable with no B2 reject predicate to author.

retireReason: Accepted+documented residual / B5 deliverable: kernel payment-blindness is by design (DECISIONS #30 routes the txid binding to the wallet/B5 layer). No B2 reject predicate exists to pin — the corresponding B2 positive (payment-agnostic acceptance) is already discharged by X10's own positive vectors.
attackFlagText: Free-floating authorization extraction: the §5 digest excludes the carrying txid, so a mature-sale authorization signed for tx A can be ripped out and mined in tx B without the payment. #30 assigns the defense to the wallet layer ('seller authorization must be bound to the exact Bitcoin transaction that pays the seller' via cooperative PSBT + a seller-controlled input). The kernel cannot detect underpayment by design — B5's transfer flow must ship that binding or mature sales are unsafe; keep this boundary loudly documented.

rationale: The flag notes the only authority is the candidate state diagram plus 'once a name is final', with no explicit MUST forbidding transfer of a provisional claim. rationale: The flag notes DECISIONS #18 names an 'explicit preserve signal' with no defined carrier and no flag-bit registry exists. This is exactly the kernel's fail-closed posture on absent spec (preserve is explicitly NOT the default per #18), which the gate guidance marks derivable now even though the eventual carrier format is future work. flagSnippet: 'Same bidder' is decided solely by bidderCommitment equality, and bidderCommitment
rationale: The same-bidder rebid rule keys on bidderCommitment (WIRE_FORMAT §6: sha256 over arbitrary uncommitted bidderId text bound to no key/sig/UTXO). The flag identifies that one actor evades the rule with fresh bidderIds — the docs have NO definition of bidder identity. Needed-spec-work and the Gaps section say a named spec PR must either bind bidderCommitment to something unforgeable or restate replacement as purely outpoint-based; until then the rule constrains only honest self-identifiers. The corrective verdict requires inventing a bidder-identity definition. The doc itself only pins the documented-hole vector (both bids accepted as written), which is a candidate-stays gameable-behavior pin, not a derivable corrective reject.

flagSnippet: A non-qualifying AuctionBid is neither a qualifying bond nor a claim — no doc
rationale: The null-effect of a below-floor/non-qualifying bid is derivable from ratified authority: GLOSSARY 'a qualifying bond (at or above the bond floor) is the only thing that opens an auction (#37)' and Decision #37 'One cheap claim, no bond by the deadline -> finalizes' and 'zero qualifying bonds' nullification rule, plus LAUNCH 'no auction exists until a valid bonded opening bid confirms.' A below-floor bid is not a qualifying bond and is not a claim, so the notice-window outcome is computed as if it never confirmed. The verdicts (single cheap claim + below-floor bid -> claim finalizes; below-floor bond-first -> unowned/unclaimed) follow directly — a non-qualifying bid neither opens an auction nor counts as a collision. No invention.

flagSnippet: The witnessed-evidence input format is a B3 deliverable that does
rationale: Although the witnessed-evidence FORMAT is a B3 deliverable, the verdict this flag demands is the kernel's fail-closed posture on absent/unverifiable evidence — per the task gate, a flag purely about fail-closed posture on absent evidence IS derivable now even though the evidence format is B3. Ratified SOFTWARE_CANON pins it: pure predicate over witnessed inputs, 'No DB, no network, no clock', evidence layer can never override a kernel verdict, and an absent witness must fail closed (DA §6e S4 candidate corroborates). A boolean (available: yes/no) whose provenance is unverifiable re-creates the callback hole and must be a conformance failure — derivable today.
